name: deployment_release_gate
description: Unattended DevOps release gate. Reads a release manifest and a list of microservices, runs pre-flight checks (approval, rollback plan, change-freeze window), then for each service deterministically pulls CI/health/incident metrics from the DevOps API and classifies a gate outcome (promote / canary / hold / block / not_found / error). Tallies the batch, notifies on blockers, promotes the green services (canarying first-time ones), and writes a dated release report. If a pre-flight check fails it aborts the whole release and writes an abort report. Never asks the user anything.

variables.description: Path to the release manifest JSON (release_id, target_environment, approval, budgets, min_services_required). Default: examples/deployment_release_gate/config/release.json.
Path to the plain-text file holding one microservice name per line to gate. Default: examples/deployment_release_gate/services.txt.
Whether the release manifest is approved for the target environment.
Whether the manifest documents a rollback plan.
Whether a global production change freeze is currently in effect.
The list of per-service decided records collected by the gate_services listDecision step.
How many services received a promote or canary outcome.
How many services received a block, hold, error, or not_found outcome.
How many services received a canary outcome (first-time deploy, no baseline).
Minimum number of promotable services required for the release to proceed.

settings.action: Read the release manifest JSON at `examples/deployment_release_gate/config/release.json`. Extract release_id, target_environment, change_ticket, the `approved` flag, the rollback_plan text, error_budget_pct, latency_budget_ms, and min_services_required. Set release_approved from `approved`, set rollback_plan_present to true when rollback_plan is non-empty, and set min_services_required from the manifest. Do not ask any questions; this is an unattended batch run.
Confirm the manifest is safe to act on for a production change: a change_ticket is present and a rollback plan is documented. This is a paperwork/pre-flight gate, not a deploy.

Query the DevOps API change-freeze endpoint `http://localhost:4100/v1/freeze` and read its JSON. Set freeze_active from the response's `freeze_active` field. A freeze means no production changes are allowed right now.
For each microservice in the release, pull its CI / health / incident metrics from the DevOps API and classify a release-gate outcome.

itemVariables.description: true if the metrics lookup failed, timed out, or returned a non-200 / unreadable response.
true if the service is not registered in the deploy catalog.
true if the latest CI pipeline for the service did not pass.
true if the service has one or more open SEV incidents.
true if the service's error rate exceeds the error budget.
true if the service's p95 latency exceeds the latency budget.
true if the service has never been deployed before (no prior baseline).
The service's current error rate, percent.
The service's current p95 latency in milliseconds.
Number of open SEV incidents on the service.
true when the outcome needs human attention (block / hold / error).
A short human-readable note explaining the gate decision.

settings.action: Read the decided records in the `gate_results` slot. Set promotable_count to the number of records whose outcome is `promote` or `canary`. Set canary_count to the number whose outcome is `canary`. Set blocker_count to the number whose outcome is `block`, `hold`, `error`, or `not_found`. Do not change any records; only compute these counts.

Compose a release-blocker notification for the on-call channel: list each service whose outcome is block, hold, error, or not_found together with its note, and page on-call for any service that has an open SEV incident. This summarizes what is holding the release back before the green services are promoted.

Promote every service whose outcome is `promote` to the target environment using the release's blue/green strategy. Record which services were promoted. Leave canary, hold, block, error and not_found services untouched.

Start a canary rollout for every service whose outcome is `canary` (first-time deploys with no baseline): route a small percentage of traffic, then record that the canary was started. Do not full-promote canary services in this run.

Build a release report object with a `summary` block at the top — release_id and target_environment from the manifest, `total` (number of gate records), `promotable` (promotable_count), `canaried` (canary_count), `blocked` (blocker_count), and a `counts` map of how many records fall under each `outcome` value — followed by `results` (the full list of gate records from `gate_results`, each with service, outcome, error_rate, p95_latency_ms, open_sev_incidents, note, and needs_attention where set). Write this object as pretty JSON to a file named `release-report-<today>.json` in the current working directory, where <today> is today's date in YYYY-MM-DD format (e.g. release-report-2026-06-22.json). Do not ask any questions; this is an unattended batch run.

The release was aborted at a pre-flight or tally gate (not approved, missing rollback plan, an active change freeze, or too few promotable services). Write an abort report as pretty JSON to a file named `release-abort-<today>.json` in the current working directory (<today> = today's date in YYYY-MM-DD), capturing the reason the release was aborted, the release_id and target_environment from the manifest if available, and any gate_results collected so far. Do not ask any questions; this is an unattended batch run.
